title: Centring half a kilo of stoneware on the wheel
subtitle: The foundational move that every thrown piece starts with
excerpt: Centring is the act of pushing a mound of clay into a state where it rotates without wobbling under the hands. It is the first technique a wheel thrower learns and the one that takes the longest to feel natural. This tutorial covers half a kilo of smooth stoneware, which is a manageable beginner weight.
sourceNotes: Wheel centring technique derived from first principles. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), pottery entry.

content.text: Centring is the gate every thrower passes through before anything else happens. A mound that is not centred will wobble under the hands, fight every forming move, and produce an uneven wall. Half a kilo of smooth stoneware is the standard beginner weight: heavy enough to feel the clay's momentum on the wheel, light enough to respond to hand pressure without needing brute force. Access to a potter's wheel is required for this technique.
Before you sit at the wheel
Wedge 500 g of smooth stoneware for five minutes; a proper
wedging
session, not a quick press. Slice the wedged block in half and check the cross-section: no air pockets, no uneven moisture. Recombine and compress into a ball. Attach a
bat
to the wheel head if one is available; if not, wet the wheel head and slap the clay ball onto the centre directly.
Securing the clay to the wheel

With the wheel stationary, press the clay ball onto the wheel head or bat with both palms. Compress downward and outward simultaneously; the clay needs to grip the surface. Spin the wheel at medium-high speed before starting to centre: the wheel is doing work; the hands are guiding.
The centring motion
1. Brace the elbows

Sit close to the wheel with the elbows locked into the thighs or body. The braced elbows make the arms rigid; the hands then apply force without wobbling themselves. Unbraced arms produce uneven, tiring pressure.
2. The cone-up move

With the wheel spinning, place both palms against the lower sides of the clay mound. Squeeze from the sides and push upward simultaneously; the clay rises into a cone shape. This first move dislodges the clay from any off-centre position. Add water with a sponge if the clay surface begins to drag.
3. The cone-down move

Place the heel of the right hand (or left hand for left-hand throwers) on the top of the cone and push down firmly while the left hand steadies the side. The cone collapses back into a lower, wider mound. Repeat: cone up, cone down. Each repetition compresses and moves the clay closer to
centring
What it looks like when it is right

A centred mound feels still under the palms. Hold both hands against the spinning clay without pushing and it should feel like touching a stationary object: no vibration, no pulsing. Looking at the mound from the side, the top profile should be flat and steady, not oscillating. Touch one fingertip to the side of the mound: a centred piece leaves a perfectly smooth line; an off-centre mound leaves a spiral groove.
Common mistakes
Gripping the clay instead of pressing it. Gripping introduces a side-to-side wobble rather than removing it. The hands apply even outward pressure from the sides, not a pinch.
Not using enough water. Dry clay drags the hands. Keep the surface lubricated but not soupy; excess water weakens the clay faster than it helps.

Working the clay for too long. Overworked clay becomes too soft to open. Centring should take two to four minutes on half a kilo; if it takes much longer, the technique needs practice, not more time on the same mound.
Diagram note

A side-view diagram of the centring sequence shows four states. First, the off-centre mound with a clear spiral wobble on the side wall. Second, the cone-up position, with both palms against the lower sides. Third, the cone-down position, with the right heel pressing the top. Fourth, the centred mound, with a flat level top and a smooth side. The elbows-braced-on-thighs position holds across all four.

items.fix: Re-secure the clay: stop the wheel, press firmly downward-and-outward with both palms before restarting
items.cause: Clay was compressed onto the wheel head with insufficient force at the start
items.symptom: Clay wobbles back immediately after being centred

items.fix: Lock both elbows against the thighs; let the body, not the arms, absorb the force
items.cause: Elbows not braced against the body

items.fix: Slow the wheel slightly and keep the clay surface wet during centring
items.cause: Clay not wetted sufficiently, or wheel speed too high for the force applied
